title: About
text:
I am an Assistant Professor in the Department of Social and Political Sciences at the University of Milan and affiliated with the Institute of Political Science at Leiden University (currently on leave). I serve as co-editor of Qualitative & Multi-Method Research, the biannual publication of APSA’s Qualitative and Multi-Method Research Section, and I am a member of the Editorial Board of the International Studies Review. I am also a member of the Amsterdam Conflict Research Network (ACRN).
My research integrates different methods and forms of evidence, often incorporating extensive fieldwork, to examine political and criminal violence. I am particularly interested in the decision-making processes of individuals and communities living in violent settings and in how their experiences with violence shape their political preferences, including attitudes toward different policy responses to violence and crime. I have studied these issues in countries affected by civil war and organized crime, including Colombia, Mexico, Brazil, Ecuador, and Italy.
Before joining the University of Milan and Leiden University, I was a Lecturer in the Department of Politics and International Relations (DPIR) and an Associate Member of Nuffield College at the University of Oxford. I was previously a Postdoctoral Fellow at the Bremen International Graduate School of Social Science (BIGSSS) and a Research Fellow at the Program on Order, Conflict and Violence (OCV) at Yale University. I received my Ph.D. from the European University Institute.
For my full CV, click here.
You can reach me at: juan.masullo@unimi.it

title: Juan Masullo
text:
Juan Masullo
Political Scientist
and passionate rock climber
I am an Assistant Professor of Political Science at the University of Milan and affiliated with Leiden University. My research examines political and criminal violence, civilian agency, and resistance, with a particular focus on Latin America.
Using a range of methods, I study how individuals and communities respond to armed actors and how experiences with violence shape political attitudes and behavior.
